titleAr: ألا بكرت عرسي توائم من لحى
titleEn: My wife rose early to reproach me

verses.sadr: ألا بكرت عرسي توائم من لحى

verses.ajuz: رأى ثوبه يوما من الدهر فاكتسى
وأعلن أخرى إن تراخت بك النوى
غوى أمر كعب ما أراد وما ارتأى
بني ملقط عني إذا قيل : من عنى

verses.sadr: فما خلتكم يا قوم كنتم أذلة

verses.ajuz: إذا لدغت لم تشف لدغتها الرقى

verses.sadr: فإن تغضبوا أو تدركوا لي بذمة
لقد نال زيد الخيل مال أخيكم
verses.ajuz: وأصبح زيد بعد فقر قد اقتنى
وما بالكميت من خفاء لمن رأى
يبين إذا ما قيد في الخيل أو جرى

verses.sadr: شديد الشظى عبل الشوى شنج النسا
verses.ajuz: كأن مكان الردف من ظهره وعى

translationEn: My wife rose early to rebuke me — how close are women's dreams to ruin! She blamed me for the sake of Bakr — by my life, her blame was twice-given. Do not blame a naked man who, finding his garment one day, dressed himself. I swear, had I not wished to keep a secret and proclaim another when distance draws you near... Men said they care nothing for our affairs — Ka'b has strayed from what he sought. For a while you dwelt between us, she-gazelles bright of flank. A traveler: if you pass, then convey my message to the sons of Mulaqat — say who sent it. I never imagined you, O people, were abased, nor that you could be robbed of your harvest. You were a serpent on the plain and the ridge — whose bite no charm could cure. If you are wrathful or would honor a pledge, by your life, such effort is sufficient. Zayd of the horses has taken your brother's wealth and grown rich after poverty. The sorrel horse holds a bond at Zayd's, and nothing about that horse is hidden. It makes clear to the great men of men's ranks, and becomes apparent when bridled among horses or when it gallops. Taut like the wolf of the thicket, shod, its hooves do not bleed from the gravel. Strong of thigh, thick of shanks, taut of sinew — it is as if where the rider sits on its back is a protected hollow.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rising early, setting out at dawn
the outer bones of the leg (in a horse)

context: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr addresses his wife's reproaches while boasting of tribal bonds and praising a horse given to Zayd al-Khayl, reflecting his pre-Islamic and early Islamic tribal loyalties.

titleEn: Why did you not ask, not being ignorant

verses.ajuz: وشفاء ذي العي السؤال عن العمى

verses.sadr: عن مشهدي ببعاث إذ دلفت له
ما إن وجدت له فداء غيره

translationEn: Why did you not ask — you who are no fool — for the cure of ignorance is to ask questions? Ask about my station at the Battle of Bu'ath, when the Ghassanids advanced with bright cutting swords and spears. Ask about my steadfast grappling in a battle where young men contested bravery. I bought him from a dark thick thicket, standing at 'Ukaz market in the morning. I found no ransom for him other than that — such was their ransom in times past. I am a man who guards his honor; my nature is nobility and avoidance of shame. I come from a people among whom are great lords, lions of the thickets when war blazes. Every radiant warrior charges with his body, like a meteor when it kindles among the firewood.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a famous pre-Islamic battle between the Aws and Khazraj

context: Ka'b boasts of his valor at the pre-Islamic Battle of Bu'ath and his noble lineage, asserting his bravery and chivalric character to a woman who questioned him.

titleEn: If death or old age overtakes you
translationEn: If death or grey old age overtakes you, know that before you, nations have died and greyed. We tarried and outlived men who were called — and when the people are called, they answer. Our path is the path of a people who were present and then absent, whom we saw the matter after. Do not ask — every mother will be bereaved when brothers grow many and become good.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we preceded, we left behind (as forerunners)
to be bereaved of a child

context: A brief wisdom poem reflecting on the universality of death and ageing, counseling acceptance of mortality as the common lot of humankind.

titleEn: From a desolate ruin worn by time

verses.sadr: فلم يبق فيها غير أس مذعذع
verses.ajuz: ولا من أثافي الدار إلا صليبها

verses.sadr: وإذ هي كغصن البان خفاقة الحشى
فدعها وعد الهم عنك ولو دعا
verses.ajuz: إلى ذكر سلمى كل يوم طروبها

verses.sadr: أتصبو إلى سلمى ومن دون أهلها

translationEn: From a desolate ruin worn by time — do your eyes pour forth floods of tears? Ravaged by long decay after its newness, the south winds dragged their skirts across it. Nothing remained but a crumbling foundation, and from the hearth-stones only their crosses. Its people bore away from it and drifted far, carried off by the bitterness of departure and death. She was once like a willow branch, heart-fluttering, her beauty and fragrance startling you. What remains of the love between her and me are wishes sent by a deceiver. Leave her, turn grief away — though every day its eager ones call you to the memory of Salma. Do you yearn for Salma when between you and her people lie deserts that swallow the camels? My father and my clan bade me be forgiving, and to defend her in matters that trouble. Preserve love among your people, and shield yourself from what would shame you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the three stones of a cooking hearth
its longing ones, those stirred by desire

context: A classical nasib (prelude) in which Ka'b mourns an abandoned campsite and the memory of his beloved Salma, before counseling himself to patience and forbearance.

titleAr: ما برح الرسم الذي بين حنجر
titleEn: The ruin between Hanjir still remains

verses.sadr: ما برح الرسم الذي بين حنجر
verses.ajuz: وذلفة حتى قيل هل هو نازح
وما بيع من يبتاع مثلي رابح

verses.sadr: الا ليت سلمى كلما حان ذكرها
وقالت تعلم أن ما كان بيننا
فلما قضينا من منى كل حاجة
verses.ajuz: ومسح ركن البيت من هو ماسح
ولا ينظر الغادي الذي هو رائح
بدا قارح منه ولم يبد قارح

verses.sadr: أخو الأرض يستخفي بها غير أنه
verses.ajuz: اذا استاف منها قارحا فهو صائح

translationEn: The ruin between Hanjir and Dhulfa has not vanished — to the point it was said: has it receded? You kept hoping for Su'da's goodness and love, drawing farther away until the grey spread in your temples. Until you saw a figure growing in likeness to yourself, and half your head is bare and white. White crept over my brows like gazelles passing, some auspicious, some ominous. I buy nothing now without consultation — and no merchant profits who buys from one like me. Would that whenever Salma is recalled, the blowing winds could carry my words to her. She said: know that what was between us is yours to receive — your covenant is sound. My trust will fully deliver it to you, as gifts after the weaning are returned. She said: know that some of my in-laws and my husband are all wrathful toward you, harboring enmity. They sharpen blades with their hands, every one of them, who would slit your throat if they could. A stirring procession of litters beautified upon them — I sought it while the surge of youth still raced in me. When we had fulfilled every need at Mina, and whoever rubbed the corner of the House did so, and the saddles were strapped on the humps of the she-camels, departing travelers not looking back — we set forth on the free-striding camels and the deserts and hard plains cast us about. We drew out the threads of conversation between us as the flat grounds bent the camels' necks. I flew to a long-necked she-camel whose neck led her shoulders, her sides tightened. It was as if I dressed the saddle with a four-year-old dark beast, that the valley of Rajaa and its wide spaces enclosed — compact as the knot of a rope-maker, solid, a tested one appearing in it yet another not apparent. As if it wore beneath a quilted lining, its collar-opening and seams parting. A creature of the earth, hiding in it, yet when it scents a tested stallion, it cries out. Its resting-place was the soft grounds of 'Amir's soft grounds, and the hot summer winds stirred above it from the Scorpion star.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ruin, old trace of a campsite
a she-camel with a long neck
hot summer winds from the north

context: A long qasida combining a nasib recounting a secret encounter with his beloved near Mecca during pilgrimage, and an extended description of his fast she-camel, characteristic of the classical ode structure.

titleEn: We struck at dawn the clan of Banu Jihash

verses.sadr: فإن تك أخطأت سعد بن بكر

translationEn: We struck at dawn the clan of Banu Jihash with a cunning, weighty calamity. They did not flee that morning, but the fire of battle raged among them and they could not resist. If you missed Sa'd ibn Bakr, you have left their allies enslaved — the sons of 'Awf and Dahman ibn Nasr. God does what He wills. We struck them at dawn with a force of a thousand, their water-camels churning the water-bags. It seized them at the ankles while seeking the shepherds of sheep and the short-fleeced rams. We surged and then drew back, holding our ground for whoever wished to fight. With blows that the hyenas would mate from, their mates coming to them and seeking copulation again.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we came upon them at dawn (in a raid)

context: A war poem in which Ka'b boasts of a dawn raid against the clan of Banu Jihash, describing the battle and the prowess of his forces.

titleAr: أبت ذكرة من حب ليلى تعودني
titleEn: The memory of Layla's love returns to haunt me

verses.sadr: أبت ذكرة من حب ليلى تعودني
verses.ajuz: عياد أخي الحمى إذا قلت أقصرا

verses.sadr: ألم تعلمي أني إذا وصل خلة

verses.ajuz: نضت عن أديم ليلة الطل أحمرا

verses.sadr: امين الشظى عبل إذا القوم آنسوا
verses.ajuz: مدى العين شخصا كان بالشخص أبصرا

verses.sadr: تركت به من آخر الليل موضعي
على عجل مني غشاشا وقد بدا

translationEn: The memory of love for Layla keeps visiting me like a fever-brother — whenever I say 'stop,' it returns. It is as if at the heights of the noble ground and 'Aqil, the tops of palms rise up and pitched ships appear. Did you not know that when a bond of friendship is severed, I am more worthy of patience? A dense meadow dripping with dew, whose fly was like a wine-lover who felt longing and remembered. I descended with a saddled beast whose trappings had been peeled from the moist leather of a red rainy night. Sound of thigh-bones, thick-built — when people spotted a figure in the distance, it was keener-eyed than that figure. My wide-flank horse, I watered it for the people and they drank from their skins from the stagnant yellow water. A vast wilderness — the mounted beast groaned to traverse it; when people watered the unknown one, it left. Along its two sides the weak camels and its spine stood up, twisting the groaning, twisted groan. I left my resting place at the end of the night, near it, where my layover met the hammered pegs. Two lean, compact, twisted she-camels, like the sheath of a Yemeni blade whose iron had worn away. A lofty, long-necked lookout — I hurried to a resting point to be consoled by shapes or to light a fire. In my haste, briefly — and the tops of palms appeared and the day reddened and receded.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a horse with a saddle-cloth (from لبن, padding)

context: A qasida beginning with the memory of Layla and moving into a journey narrative featuring the desert, his horse, and a dangerous waterless passage — a typical classical ode structure.

titleEn: My wife has finally given me warning

verses.sadr: يوم صوم من الظهيرة أو يو
مثل درص اليربوع لم يرب عنه

translationEn: My wife has finally warned me — she did not delay nor consult a leader. Was it departure she prepared — no reproach in it — or did she intend betrayal and immorality? What good are two spouses living together after the great one severs from the great one? Be patient as I have been patient — a noble person is always patient. What time is this, when we have both aged, having lived ages upon ages? We say nothing but what is repeated, re-said and recycled in our speech. You blamed me, I said do not — I have gone out at dawn to the blamed and hung-over man, a morning man, and I found with him nothing but a nagging woman growling with bitterness. I blamed him until he said 'leave me — I will restrain my thinking.' She was distracted and saw only a one bearing a spirit that kicks at the slain. Leave me from blame — it is enough for me that sometimes I seek the watering-places of lies... [The poem continues with an extended description of a wild ass and a hunter, the watering hole, and the arrow's flight, across many verses of elaborate nature poetry].

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she gave me notice, warned me

context: Ka'b's longest poem in this collection, beginning with a domestic scene with his wife then transitioning into an extended wasf (description) of a wild ass, a hunt at a waterhole, and an archer — a tour de force of classical descriptive poetry.

titleAr: لو كنت أعجب من شيء لأعجبني
titleEn: Were I to marvel at anything, this would amaze me

verses.sadr: لو كنت أعجب من شيء لأعجبني
verses.ajuz: سعي الفتى وهو مخبوء له القدر

verses.sadr: والمرء ما عاش ممدود له أمل
verses.ajuz: لا تنتهي العين حتى ينتهي الأثر

translationEn: Were I to marvel at anything, the striving of a young man would amaze me — while fate lies hidden for him. The young man strives for matters he cannot attain, with but one soul and worries scattered. A man, as long as he lives, has hope stretched before him — the eye does not cease until the footprint ceases.
context: A short wisdom poem (hikma) meditating on the futility of human striving against fate and the nature of hope — concise and philosophically resonant.

titleEn: Pause at the campsite of Dhat al-Mazahir

verses.ajuz: وما هو عن حي القنان بسائر

verses.sadr: ولما أجن الليل نقبا ولم أخف
verses.ajuz: على أثر مني ولا عين ناظر

translationEn: Pause at the campsite of Dhat al-Mazahir, remaining worn like the threads of a cloak. The winds alternate over it — its people have departed, yet it does not drift from the habitations of Qinan. A fire before dawn — I rushed to strike its flint — the fire greeting, lit for a traveler. Its provisions glowed within it, its scouts posted on a high lookout dominating the ridges. When night darkened a pass and I feared neither my trace nor a watching eye, I took my weapons and descended to a man of little harm whose chest harbors no malice. I flew with my saddle and he took the same upon a lean she-camel, like a grave-post emaciated. She avoids the creak of the saddle and shields herself with something like the flat stones of the facing valley. By morning it was an evening's distance away, its mountains looking from afar like the bare necks of unveiled women.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a campsite, a quarter (where a tribe rested)
women with uncovered necks or arms

context: Ka'b pauses at an abandoned campsite and narrates a night raid — describing the fire, the armed descent, and the swift flight on lean camels across the desert.

titleEn: By your life, but for God's mercy I would

verses.sadr: فلو كنت حوتا ركض الماء فوقه
verses.ajuz: ولو كنت يربوعا سرى ثم قصعا

verses.sadr: إذا ما نتجنا أربعا عام كفأة
إذا قلت إني في بلاد مضلة

translationEn: By your life, but for God's mercy, I would strain hard at whatever it desires to lift me. Were I a fish, the water would run over it; were I a jerboa, it would roam then burrow. When we produce four young in a lean year, the weasels seek them and destroy all four. Whenever I say I am in a land of confusion, it refuses — for our evening and morning are together.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: it burrowed, dug into the earth
a place of confusion, a trackless desert

context: A brief poem reflecting on divine providence and fate, using animal imagery of fish, jerboa, and predators to illustrate the inescapability of God's mercy and decree.

titleEn: I journeyed to my people to call most of them

verses.sadr: فإن أنتم لم تفعلوا ما أمرتكم
فأوف بما عاهدت بالخيف من منى
verses.ajuz: أبا النصر إذ سدت عليك المطالع

translationEn: I journeyed to my people to summon most of them to a matter of wisdom bound by solid agreements — to fulfill what they pledged at the valley of Mina, while God watches and hears. To join the bonds of kinship, relieve the debtor, and restore the old affection to those who bring it back. Convey this to all the clans of Uthman and the clan of Aws — convey what I am doing. I will call them earnestly to righteousness and piety, and to nobility as long as my fingers follow me. Be united as best you can — God will clothe you in a vast garment. Rise and care for your people, gather them, and be a hand that builds glory and defends. If you do not do what I command, then at least honor your pledges — for covenants are trusts. How different is he who calls and fulfills his pledge from him who breaks the confirmed covenant. To you, Abu Nasr, my counsel has traveled — the submissive camels carry it from me. Honor what you pledged at the valley of Mina, Abu Nasr, when the passes closed upon you. We are the sons of the elders — you know it — we defend our honor and hold our ground. We station defenders at the feared frontier to relieve distress or to feed the hungry.
context: A poem urging tribal reconciliation and fulfillment of pledges made at Mina during the pilgrimage, directed to Abu Nasr and the clans of Uthman and Aws, reflecting Ka'b's role as a counselor to his people.

titleAr: بان الشباب وأمسى الشيب قد أزفا
titleEn: Youth has departed and old age has drawn near

verses.sadr: بان الشباب وأمسى الشيب قد أزفا

verses.ajuz: لا مرحبا هابذا اللون الذي ردفا

verses.sadr: في كل يوم أرى منه مبينة

verses.ajuz: بل ليته ارتد منه بعض ما سلفا

verses.sadr: ما شرها بعد ما ابيضت مسائحها
verses.ajuz: لا الود أعرفه منها ولا اللطفا

verses.sadr: لو أنها آذنت بكرا لقلت لها
verses.ajuz: يا هيد مالك أو لو آذنت نصفا

verses.sadr: لولا بنوها وقول الناس ما عطفت
verses.ajuz: على العتاب وشر الود ما عطفا
في غير نائرة ضبا لها شنفا

verses.sadr: سمح درير اذا ما صوة عرضت

verses.ajuz: حتى يؤوب سمالا قد خلت خلفا
ما ضارب الدف من جنانها عزفا

verses.sadr: أبقى التهجر منها بعد ما ابتذلت
يجتاز أرض فلاة غير أن بها

verses.ajuz: في الآل مخلولة في قرطف شرفا
لا يألوان من التنوم ما نقفا
بعض العذاب فجالا بعد ما كتفا
لا يحقران من الخطبان ما نقفا
حتى رأته وقد أوفى لها شرفا

translationEn: Youth has departed and old age has drawn near at evening — I see no replacement for departing youth. Black has turned to white at the temples — no welcome to this color that has followed! Each day I see from it a clear sign, one that nearly makes my strength fall from me in sorrow. Would that youth were an ally who never left us — or would that some of what has passed could return. She has changed after her temples whitened — I recognize neither the love in her nor the gentleness. Had she given warning in the morning, I would have said: what is wrong? Or had she warned in midlife... Were it not for her sons and what people would say, I would not have turned to reproach — the worst of love is what turns to it. I will always, even if I flatter, harbor suppressed anger — without cause, a resentment that wounds her. [The poem continues with an extended description of a sandgrouse, its nesting, foraging, its encounter with an ostrich and then a falcon — a lengthy descriptive section in the tradition of classical Arabic nature poetry].

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the temples (sides of the head where hair grows)

context: A lengthy qasida opening with Ka'b's lament over lost youth and a difficult relationship, then transitioning into a celebrated description of sand-grouse and an ostrich, showcasing his mastery of animal description.

titleEn: How did the phantom come to haunt you, circling

verses.sadr: فعزفت عنها إنما هو أن أرى
لا هالك جزعا على ما فاتني
أو حرف حنو من غبيط ذابل

translationEn: How did the phantom come to haunt you and circle — its circling being a memory and passion for you? It brings me needs at night, bewildering me — all from the family of Khawla is well-known. I spent the night in its presence as if surrendered to jinn, my heart startled and snatched. I turned away from it — for what I see and cannot attain, I am one who turns away. Not perishing in grief over what has passed me by, and accustomed to whatever calamities befall. A golden-complexioned girl, whose conversation brings comfort to her like — one who heals the thirst of a longing heart. Had she been generous toward an eagle in a well-fortified refuge, lofty below the sky, a long-necked kohl-eyed woman with wide eyes, whose shoulder-strap the autumn watered, would have drawn him down. Leave her, and seek her pursuit with a noble she-camel when departure and haste have come upon you — a lean-bodied she-camel inherited by travelers, her body bare, moving with you while the heart is snatched. The place of the saddle on her back is like a sword whose sheath has grown old and hollow. Or a curved rib of a saddle-frame, its wood bent, a smith-woman having shaped it. When you raise the right rein she veers away from the crooked gap among them as the opening passes. Her complaint when she climbs the highlands after exhaustion is creaking and groaning. As if my saddle on her worn body is an emaciated creature its flesh worn by delay — like a bow stripped for sale by a herdsman, or a lance straightened by the pressing-frame. Is it she or a spotted grey she-ass, bare of haunches, accurate in her evening pace, browsing? With a white belly-stripe within, her back-fur being two colors, dappled. She spent the day watching her mate, their desire a valley lush in summer. The male ass escapes with her, hollow-boned, as if with his nose-ring and halter he is an earring-wearer. His poll is bare, from his chest flies fluff stirred by the wind, thin. It is as if she is a Nubian woman and he a man from her own people, captivated.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the creaking sound of saddle or teeth

context: A qasida beginning with a nocturnal phantom vision of his beloved, moving to a description of his lean she-camel, and ending with a vivid picture of a wild ass and his mate — showcasing Ka'b's three-part classical ode structure.

titleEn: The day of Wajj expelled the people of Hablaqa
translationEn: The day of Wajj expelled the people of Hablaqa — Muzayna openly and the sons of Khafaf. We struck them at dawn with a thousand from Sulaym and a full thousand from the sons of Uthman. They drove them from their shoulders with blows and thrusting, and shooting with the finely feathered arrows. We shot at them with young men and old, each one deterring those who would resist the embrace. You could see between the battle-lines their volleys like the hissing of arrows flying from the feathers. You could see the sleek fine horses shining among them with spears straightened by the press. We left victorious with what we desired, while they left regretting their defiance. We gave the Messenger of God our pledges of sincere reconciliation. We passed through the heart of Mecca protected by the fear of God and the light sharp swords. Our tent-pole settled in the private quarters of Najd — at Uliya, then al-Qudus, to Sharaf. They desired al-Lat and al-'Uzza as gods — God suffices against al-Lat, sufficient is He.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an ancient name for the region of Ta'if

context: A poem celebrating a military victory on the day of Wajj (near Ta'if), describing the dawn attack by a combined force of Sulaym and Uthman's tribe, followed by pledges to the Prophet Muhammad and Islamic monotheism.

titleEn: From Nawar's campsite you recognized the worn abode

verses.ajuz: إذ لا تفارق بطن الجو فالبرقا
لو أن منزل حي دارسا نطقا

verses.sadr: لا زالت الريح تزجي كل ذي لجب
verses.ajuz: عيثا إذا ما ونته ديمة دفقا

verses.sadr: فلم تزل كل غناء البغام به
تقرو به منزل الحسناء إذ رحلت

verses.ajuz: إلا صموت السرى لا تسأم العنقا
لا تشتكي للحفا من خفها رققا

verses.sadr: ترى المريء كنصل السيف إذ ضمنت

verses.ajuz: حاذ يمان إذا ما أرقلت خفقا

verses.sadr: شهم يكب القطا الكدري مختضب ال
verses.ajuz: أظفار حر ترى في عينه زرقا

verses.sadr: حتى إذا ما انجلت ظلماء ليلته

verses.ajuz: فانقض وهو بوشك الصيد قد وثقا
نفسا بما سوف ينجيها وإن لحقا
ببطن لينة ماء لم يكن رنقا
أمثل عشقي يلاقي كل من عشقا
هندا فقد علق الأحشاء ما علقا

verses.sadr: كم دونها من عدو ذي مكاشحة
ذي نيرب نزع لو قد نصبت له
verses.ajuz: وجهي لقد قال كنت الحائن الحمقا

verses.sadr: كالكلب لا يسأم الكلب الهرير ولو

verses.ajuz: أجزت غصته من بعد ما شرقا

translationEn: From Nawar's campsite you recognized the worn abode — when it did not leave the valley-floor nor the bright ground. I stopped there briefly, just long enough to question it — then my tears poured down my cheeks in a cascade. It nearly revealed through signs some of our need, if only a ruined abode of the living could speak. May the wind forever drive over it every cloud of thunder — when a steady rain is slack, let it pour. Its rain caused the rue and basil to grow, the iyahqan and muknan and dharqa. Every sweet-bellied bleat of gazelles stayed to watch, tending a sleeping herd in the open. They trace within it the campsite of the beautiful one when she departed and headed for the broad valley and its deep reaches. Nawar has settled in a land that only the night-silent swift she-camel can reach, not tiring of her pace. Vigorous after effort, reaching her goal — she does not complain of ground-hardness through her hoofpad's thinning. Her throat is like a sword-blade when laden, or a thin shaft stretching the neck. She shakes off her foam with something like the strap of a Yemeni saddle when she trots lightly. She escapes like a sandgrouse of the sky startled in the thicket-land, sensing a hunting falcon by night. A fierce falcon knocks over the dark sand-grouse — talons stained, a hot-tempered one, blue-eyed. He spent a night watching him, its hillocks many, while he shook the dew and moisture from himself. When the darkness of his night cleared and the whiteness of dawn spread and split — he swooped measured, diving and surprising her, plummeting sure of the catch. Nothing is more generous than she, content in spirit with what will save her even if he catches her. He startled her from death's watering-troughs and she migrated to the valley of Layna with unclouded water. Would I knew — would that the birds could tell me — does every lover meet the like of my love? When I hear of love mentioned, it reminds me of Hind — the bowels have clung to what has clung. How many an enemy lies between us, with hidden spite, showing an angry face. A slanderer — had you presented my face to him he would have said I was the unlucky fool. Like a dog — a dog never tires of barking, even if you set a dog against a crouching lion with the mange. A hard-pressed man called me and I answered — I freed his choking after he had all but drowned.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the froth/foam from a camel's mouth
the growling or barking of a dog

context: A long qasida opening with the ruins of Nawar's campsite and love for Hind, transitioning into a journey on a swift she-camel, then a detailed falcon-hunt sequence, before returning to the themes of love and enmity.

titleAr: أعلم أني متى ما يأتني قدري
titleEn: I know that whenever my fate comes to me

verses.sadr: أعلم أني متى ما يأتني قدري

translationEn: I know that whenever my fate comes to me, neither miserliness nor compassion will hold it back. While a young man delights in life and is content — suddenly the young man is surrendered to death, locked in.
context: A brief but profound wisdom poem on the inevitability of death, reflecting Ka'b's fatalistic acceptance of divine decree characteristic of early Islamic poetry.

titleEn: Old age has expelled the dark hair from his temples

verses.sadr: وأدركت ما قد قال قبلي لدهره
تبصر خليلي هل ترى من ظعائن
تربعن روض الحزن ما بين لية
إذا ما أتته من شطر جانب
بحافته من لا يصيح بمن سرى
verses.ajuz: ولا يدعي إلا بما هو صادقه

verses.sadr: وقد ينبري لي الجهل يوما وأنبري

verses.ajuz: على البعل لا يخلو ولا هي عاشقه

translationEn: Old age has expelled the dark hair from his temples, white showing in the black of his parting. The morning of a day and a night consumed my youth — time is nothing but its evenings and its dawns. I have come to experience what Zuhayr before me said to his age — and if he perishes, his words will endure. Look, my friend — do you see any litter-processions, like the date-palms of villages or like ships in their rows? They grazed the garden of the highlands between Liyya and Siyhan, their gardens lush and full. When they saw the place and its people departing, the fires of the flint ridge flickering and dying — they were told what lay between the water-channels and the soft dunes, watered by morning clouds, the nocturnal rains striking it. They set out early for a hollow whose back the wind weaves — the cranes' calling resembling the murmur of the Magi. When it came to them from one flank and moved to the other side, dust gathered its manuscripts. By its side one who does not cry out to the traveler passing by, summoned only by what is true to him. On every long-necked beast bending its curve, taking up the extra rein, or fanned by a cooling wind it matches pace. Sometimes ignorance confronts me and I stand to face a herd, like the bright whites of the noble she-camels meeting it. Three innocent-tongued and one eager, mounting the male — not separating, not in love with him either.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the bald patches, the spots from which hair has gone
a she-camel eager to mount, restless

context: Ka'b opens by mourning his greying hair and paying tribute to his famous father Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma, then describes women departing in their litters and a herd of she-camels — a classical ode combining personal elegy with nature description.

titleEn: Convey from me to Bujayr a message

verses.ajuz: فهل لك فيما قلت بالخيف هل لكا
على أي شيء ويب غيرك دلكا

verses.sadr: على خلق لم تلف أما ولا أبا
verses.ajuz: عليه ولم تدرك عليه اخا لكا

translationEn: Convey from me to Bujayr a message — are you for what I said at the valley? Are you for it? You drank with the Trusted One a full cup from which the Trusted One drank his fill, and you drank yours. You abandoned the paths of guidance and followed him — upon what, woe to you, did he lead you? Upon a character you found neither mother nor father following, nor any brother of yours did you find upon it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Bujayr ibn Zuhayr, Ka'b's brother who converted to Islam
the Trusted One — a title of the Prophet Muhammad
a full, satisfying cup (metaphor for Islam)

context: Ka'b's poem to his brother Bujayr who converted to Islam before Ka'b did — written in Ka'b's pre-Islamic period, criticizing his brother's conversion, which he later regretted after his own conversion and composed Banat Su'ad.

titleEn: Asma' has cut the bonds between us

verses.sadr: وذات العرض قد تأتي إذا ما

verses.ajuz: عن الحال التي في الدهر حالا

translationEn: Asma' has cut the bonds — the determination to depart has set out with the morning. A proud woman may come, when she decides to cut her friend's ties, with beauty. The slanderers took turns with her and changed her from the state she had always been in. Whoever does not repel the slanderers from himself — morning and evening they will seek his ruin. Leave off pursuing her and console yourself with a she-camel as if a phantom passes through her. Trustworthy, never tiring, never complaining when you burden her a day with exhaustion. The saddle on her back is as if she is a young male wild ass, turning his she-asses in groups. From those accustomed to the south slopes of 'Ayr — as if they wear sandals of tanned leather. His forehead is a target for the dark arrows, as if the vultures of their nails were filled with arrowheads. A hoarse-voiced male — you would think him a blood-price when he rages among his she-asses, roaming. Convey if you encounter a messenger: tell Abu al-Mamluh — for he has dignity! Are you senile behind you and growing old, though you have not yet tasted the calamity of our enmity? You have done nothing but threaten — your threats alone are enough as war upon them. A threat from which wombs miscarry, and from which mountains are shifted from their places. Light in the cloud — one who sees its cloud formation marvels, yet it has not dripped a drop of moisture.
context: Ka'b mourns the severance of his bond with Asma', blames slanderers, then describes his swift camel and warns enemies with threats so severe they could make mountains move — combining love poetry with tribal boasting.

titleEn: My wife rose early to blame and reproach me

verses.ajuz: بياضا عن اللون الذي كان أول

verses.sadr: أرنت من الشيب العجيب الذي رأت
verses.ajuz: وهل أنت مني ويب غيرك أمثل

verses.sadr: لنا حاجة في صرحة الحي بعدما

verses.ajuz: إذا ليس فيه ما أبين فأعقل

verses.sadr: يحب دنو الإنس منه وما به
verses.ajuz: إلى أحد يوما من الإنس منزل

verses.sadr: تقرب حتى قلت لم يدن هكذا
verses.ajuz: من الإنس إلا جاهل أو مضلل

verses.sadr: إذا ما عوى مستقبل الريح جاوبت
كسوب إلى أن شب من كسب واحد

verses.ajuz: حمي إذا ما صاف أو هو أهزل
إذا ما تمطى وجهة الريح محمل
إذا ما مشى مستكره الريح أقزل

verses.sadr: يكاد يرى مالا ترى عين واحد
verses.ajuz: يثير له ما غيب الترب معول
ألم تعلما أني من الزاد مرمل

verses.sadr: يئط إذا ما شد بالنسع من عل
مضت هجعة من آخر الليل ذبل
على أنه حي من النوم مثقل
verses.ajuz: من الأسود الساري وإن كان ثائرا

verses.sadr: ولا عينها من خشية السوط تغفل
كما طين بالضاحي من اللبن مجدل
إذا ما ثوى كعب وفوز جرول

verses.ajuz: كفيتك لا تلقى من الناس شاعرا

translationEn: My wife rose early to blame and reproach me — yet what she says is more chaste and beautiful. When she saw my head changing its color to white from the first color it was, she wailed over the astonishing grey she saw — and are you, woe to you, any better than me? I have witnessed the full cup joyfully, drinking deep before dawn and drinking my fill. A gentle, non-vulgar man contends with me over it — ahead of merchants at their goals, a mocker. When the cup overcomes him, he is not frowning or abstinent, nor does he scowl before it. My friend is not fickle, nor is he the type who blames the miser for miserliness while being miserly himself. We have a need in the open ground of the tribe, after it seemed they would depart and set out — the cup-companion among us reeling, she-camels kneeling with their saddles. A sound partridge whose fine covering we uncovered, and another in worn-out saddle-cloths draped. A trackless desert, bold, its humming beyond the sight of a jinn-stirred night like what imagination conjures — people's speech — when I heard it, there was nothing in it I could clarify or reason. I crossed it with one walking beside me, a diminished creature from the grey wolves, sometimes trotting and loping. He loves the nearness of humans to him, though nothing in him draws him to any person on any day. He approached until I said: nothing draws so near to humans like this except a fool or a misguided one. When he howled facing the wind, his hearing answered — mouth open over the provisions, wailing. A hunter from birth, growing up on a single hunt's earnings — poverty his companion, not growing wealthy. As if the smoke of the white-wood mixed with his color, seeping through him from inside and covering him. Expert in the thickets of the hunt when he trotted — he wanders, hides in hardship, and waits. You see him fat in winter, as if feverish in summer, or perhaps even leaner. His sinew was a stretched bow, and when he stretched toward the wind's direction, he was a load-camel. Thin-legged, keen of eye — when he walked against the resisting wind, he was as if lame. He almost sees what a single eye cannot see — the mattock stirs up for him what the earth has hidden. When a raven and a wolf presented themselves to me, I said: if only you knew it — don't you know I am out of provisions? A raven and a wolf watching when I might see — they raided what I imagined, yet both of them will find from me only disappointment in what they hoped. As if my two companions on the sand were crawling together — they passed us by, were it not for standing and resting. Her striking below the gravel with her breastbone, paired with lean legs no joint betrayed — a long neck swayed by the halter-rope like a palm-frond watered by a rivulet from Samiya. A lengthened resting-spot and the bends of a saddle-frame that creaks when pulled tight from above by the strap. Dark spears thirsty, separated from them after a slumber passed at the end of the night, lean — dust blew thick over them like a hanging palm-cluster over the flanks and haunches. A lean-bodied creature, downcast-eyed, afraid — when the open earth did not deliver its burden, I couched my young she-camel and watched her with my eye. I consulted my soul: which of my options should I do? Should I guard her fearing accidents — they indeed weigh on a person — or should I trust? I swore by the Merciful — nothing other than Him is the oath of a righteous man — I shall not break it: I will put the upper part of my cloak as a Muslim surrendered to the face of Him who gives life and death to people. He is the guardian of the sleeping man at night, as if dead, though he is alive and heavy with sleep — from the lion that prowls at night, even if it is enraged, with the concentrated venom on the edge of its fangs. When the two Farkadans wheeled, I urged her on — a winged archer rose, armed and unarmed. She set off swiftly, her heart not betraying her, nor her eye heedless for fear of the whip. Her trot cuts the pace of the noble she-camels — escape when the relied-upon escape is put to the test. Her hidden flesh swollen, her muscles thick like a tower plastered with daytime milk-mortar. Her breast like a flat stone, her elbow turned from the chest — twisted sinew, the most twisted. Her neck-side plump, her plaited rope-hair wet when the sweat-water overflowed her. Clear-eyed, repelling the floating mote — as if on the journey it wipes and kohls her. Who for the odes — who shapes them, who weaves them — when Ka'b and Jirwal are gone? He speaks — never stumbling over anything he says — and from those who speak them are those who do ill and labor to straighten them until their backs stand straight — every example falls short of them. I have been sufficient to you — you will not find among people a poet who sifts from them the like of what I sift.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she wailed, cried out in distress
a white-wood shrub used for fuel
the two Farkad stars of Ursa Minor

context: Ka'b's massive qasida combining a domestic opening about his wife's lament over his grey hair, a drinking session, a dangerous desert crossing accompanied by a wolf, a long camel-description, a religious oath of submission to God, and ending with a famous boast of poetic excellence after his father Zuhayr — one of the most comprehensive poems in the diwan.

titleEn: Su'ad has gone, and my heart today is stricken

verses.sadr: وما سعاد غداة البين إذ رحلوا
تجلو عوارض ذي ظلم إذا ابتسمت
شجت بذي شبم من ماء محنية
يا ويحها خلة لو أنها صدقت
verses.ajuz: ما وعدت أو لو ان النصح مقبول

verses.sadr: لكنها خلة قد سيط من دمها
فما تدوم على حال تكون بها
أرجو وآمل أن يعجلن في أبد
فلا يعرنك ما منت وما وعدت
من كل نضاخة الذفرى إذا عرقت

verses.ajuz: في خلقها عن بنات الفحل تفضيل

verses.sadr: عيرانة قذفت في اللحم عن عرض
تمر مثل عسيب النخل ذا خصل

verses.ajuz: إنك يا بن أبي سلمى لمقتول

verses.sadr: فقلت خلوا طريقي لا أبا لكم
كل ابن أنثى وان طالت سلامته
مهلا هداك الذي أعطاك نافلة ال
لقد أقوم مقاما لو يقوم به
verses.ajuz: أرى وأسمع ما لو يسمع الفيل

verses.sadr: لظل يرعد إلا أن يكون له

verses.ajuz: في كف ذي نقمات قيله القيل

verses.sadr: من ضيغم من ضراء الأسد مخدرة
إذا يساور قرنا لا يحل له
verses.ajuz: أن يترك القرن الا وهو مفلول

verses.sadr: و لا يزال بواديه أخو ثقة
في عصبة من قريش قال قائلهم

verses.ajuz: من نسيج داود في الهيجا سرابيل

verses.sadr: بيض سوابغ قد شكت لها حلق
لا يقع الطعن إلا في نحورهم
verses.ajuz: ما إن لهم عن حياض الموت تهليل

translationEn: Su'ad has gone — my heart today is stricken, enslaved in her wake, unchained. Su'ad at the morning of parting when they departed was nothing but a gazelle-eyed, downcast-glanced, kohl-eyed one. She reveals side-teeth of glistening white when she smiles — like a watering place seasoned with wine. She mixed it with cold water from a curving river, clear in a wide valley, exposed to the north wind at noon. The winds clear the motes from it, and the white swift clouds filled it from above. Woe to her — a companion, if only she had been true to her promise, or if counsel were accepted. But she is a companion in whose blood sorrow and obsession and betrayal and change have been mixed. She does not remain in a state she is in, just as the ghoul changes through her garments. She does not hold onto the bond she claimed — only as a sieve holds water. 'Arqub's promises were the byword for her — and her promises are nothing but vanities. I hope and expect them to hasten in eternity — and they have no hastening throughout all time. Do not let what she gave and promised concern you — wishes and dreams are delusion. Su'ad at evening is in a land that none can reach except the noble swift she-camels sent racing. The Messenger is a sword by which light is sought — in it at exhaustion is the brisk trot and amble. From every one who pours sweat from the ear's base when she sweats — she crosses a land with obliterated landmarks, unknown. She shoots the unseen with the eyes of a lone white she-camel when the plateaus and plains kindle. Large-necklaced, thick-fetlocked — in her form there is superiority over the daughters of stallions. Lean-bodied — her brother is her father from a mixed dam, her uncle is her maternal uncle — long-necked, swift. A tick walks on her then slips off her — her breast and flanks are smooth. Sturdy in body and thrown from the meat sideways — her elbow twisted from the breast-bones. What passes between her eyes and her dewlap from her muzzle and jawbones is like a bar of iron. Her tail passes like a palm-frond of fiber in a channel that her duct-milk has not betrayed. High-bridged in her noble bearing — to the expert there is clear nobility, and in the cheeks smoothness. She treads on light steps, lean of them — the earth makes their sound a clicking. Brown-sinewed, she leaves the pebbles scattered — the hilltops have not provided her with shoeing. A day in which the chameleon swells its throat — as if its sun-exposed side is roasted by fire. As if the back-and-forth of her elbows when she has sweated — and the mirages have wrapped the rocky hills. The camel-driver said to the people when the green locusts began to scratch the pebbles with their legs: rest at midday. The midday intensified — a long-necked she-camel at half-growth stood and a bereaved, hard-luck mournful one answered her. A loose-armed wailer — when the criers announced the death of her young, she had no restraint. She tears her breast with her hands and her robe is split from her collarbones in tatters. The tale-bearers run by her sides saying: O son of Abu Sulma, you shall be killed. Every friend I had hoped said: I will not be found — I am preoccupied with you. I said: clear my path — no father have you — for all that the Merciful has decreed will be done. Every son of a woman, however long his safety, one day will be carried on a hump-backed bier. I was told that the Messenger of God threatened me — and forgiveness from the Messenger of God is hoped for. Be gentle — may He guide you who gave you the gift of the Quran, in which are admonitions and details. I stand in a station — if the elephant were to stand in it and see and hear what I see and hear — it would keep trembling unless it had from the Messenger, by God's leave, a gift. Until I placed my right hand — not contesting him — in the palm of one of retributions, whose word is the word. He was more awe-inspiring to me when I spoke to him — and it was said: you are inspected and will be questioned. From a lion from the lion-dens, crouching in the belly of 'Athar — a thicket before which is a thicket. He goes out and feeds two lions whose life is the flesh of people, dust-covered and in tatters. When he wrestles a foe, it is not permitted him to leave the foe but shattered. From him the wild asses stay lean, and no traveling men walk through his valley. Always in his valley is a man of trust — his goods scattered and his dried bread eaten. The Messenger is a sword by which light is sought — an Indian blade from the swords of God, unsheathed. Among a band from Quraysh — their speaker said in the belly of Mecca when they embraced Islam: they departed. They departed — and they never ceased to be: not cowardly reversers, nor those who expose their backs at meeting, nor swaying unarmed men. High-nosed heroes — their armor in battle is the weaving of David, chainmail coats. White-bright complete coats that have their rings interlocked — as if they are the rings of the qaf'a' tree, braided. They walk with the gait of white camels — blows protect them when the dark base-born flee. They do not rejoice when their lances strike a people, and they are not distraught when struck. The spear falls nowhere but in their throats — they have no turning away from the watering-troughs of death.
context: The famous Banat Su'ad qasida — Ka'b's masterpiece composed upon his conversion to Islam and recited before the Prophet Muhammad who granted him forgiveness and his cloak (burda). It opens with longing for Su'ad, transitions into a description of his she-camel, then praises the Prophet as a drawn sword of God and celebrates the Companions of Quraysh.

titleEn: From Umm Shaddad the ruins of the campsites

verses.ajuz: على إثر حول قد تجرم كامل

verses.sadr: أرى أم شداد بها شبه ظبية

verses.ajuz: فما شئت من بخل ومن منع نائل

verses.sadr: وما ذاك عن شيء أكون اجترمته
verses.ajuz: سوى أن شيبا في المفارق شاملي

verses.sadr: إذا ما خليل لم يصلك فلا تقم
متى ما تشأ تسمع إذا ما هبطته
وخرق يخاف الركب أن يدلجوا به

translationEn: From Umm Shaddad are these ruins of campsites — I imagined them after a sandy drift and heavy rain. After nights that have passed and months, on the heels of a full year completed. I see in Umm Shaddad something like a gazelle, circling with kohl-heavy, downcast eyes. Soft-voiced, lowered gaze, soft-hoofed — roaming in a dark, massive sand-dune. Gazing with the eyes of a ewe-mother of a fawn, staying in the valley of a garden and shade. Stepping on two green hillocks watered by the shaking evening rains and the pourer. She parts to reveal bright front teeth — like white wood-sorrel watered from deep spreading roots. I found she had developed strange traits — all the miserliness and withholding of gift you could want. Not for any fault I committed — except that white has spread across my temples. If you sever me — woe to you — sever! I have received notice like the notice of a parting companion. When a friend does not connect with you, do not stand in his hollow ground — turn to another who does. A trackless desert guiding the lost like a mat of craft between the hands of the women weavers. Whenever you wish you can hear, when you descend into it, the murmur of a flock settling at sunset. She-sandgrouse feeding their chicks in the waste, twins — the red-chested hens broke the eggs for them. Twins alike without a mark — laid in an unknown, low stretch of land. A wilderness the riders fear to traverse by night — they bite their fingers at its terrors. Its thickets hold terrors, its wolves howl — I crossed it on a thick-armed, mature she-camel. Silent in her night-travel, mute in it, turning her head at a true sound or a false resemblance. The saddle-straps settle over her after exhaustion — their creaking between her mid-section and shoulder. High-chested and with ribs that grew with her — limbs curved, sinews raised. She answers echoes and sometimes is startled — like a hunting dog leaping at the company, frenzied. A massive, proud she-camel strutting with the saddle — matching young she-camels like ostriches in headlong flight. With a continuous sure step, without effort when she descends into soft sand, without stumbling. As if her running partner is a rearing wild ass from the bright dunes between the two gentle slopes and 'Aqil. He sings in the flat waste with a she-donkey herd — gaunt of belly, like straight swaying spears. Her young distant from her in summer — her udders have dried up like kohl-tubes. The midday hours found him resolving his matter on the hump of the al-Baha' hill with its channels. He intended to water at al-Rasis, but was turned back by men lying in wait in the dark with spear-shafts. When she came to water at night she swerved — fearing an archer or a snarer. As if colocynth rolled — where she sniffed at her resting-ground she licked with her lips.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a drift of sand blown by wind
the tear-ducts, the eyes (lit. the crying-places)

context: A qasida beginning with the ruins of Umm Shaddad's campsite and the beloved's changing character after Ka'b's ageing, transitioning into a journey narrative featuring a swift she-camel and a wild ass seeking water, ending at a dangerous night waterhole.

titleEn: Do you recognize a ruin between Rahman and al-Raqm

verses.sadr: أنا ابن الذي لم يخزني في حياته

من الدهر في ذبيان إن حوضها انهدم

verses.sadr: إذا شئت أعلكت الجموح إذا بدت

verses.ajuz: كراما بنوا لي المجد في باذخ أشم

verses.sadr: هم الأصل مني حيث كنت وإنني

قديما وهم أجلوا أباك عن الحرم

verses.sadr: متى أدع في أوس وعثمان يأتني

translationEn: Do you recognize a ruin between Rahman and al-Raqm — extending to the striated place, as if drawn with a pen? The summer winds have effaced it with their dust, and the dew of Orion with rain and flood. The abode of she who cut our bonds and severed — and I, whenever the bond of a friend was cut, would take refuge in a lean firm she-camel, as if her flanks were tar when her hide grew damp. Convey to this one who turned away: was he awake when he spoke his words, or was he dreaming? If people ask about me — I am the son of Abu Sulma, despite the spite of those who spite. I am the son of one who lived ninety years without being shamed a single day before Ma'add, without blame. His equals honored him in every noble company — if you deny me, ask the nations. His odes reached the non-Arabs and the horizons, remaining as the revealed word remains in solid stone. I am the son of one who never shamed me in his life, and I never shamed him until he was buried in his tomb. He gave until his death — wealth and ambition — and bequeathed me glory and nobility when he bid farewell. He used to defend when a hard calamity of time befell Dhubyan, if their water-trough was broken. I say things resembling what he said, knowing them — and one who resembles his father does no wrong. When you wish, I can silence the stubborn one when the back teeth of his jaws show at their coarsest. You have granted me a formidable glory and noble companions who built my glory on a high lofty peak. They are my root wherever I am — I am from the Muznis, refined by nobility. They struck you when you strayed from guidance with their swords until you stood straight on correct values. A band of Khandaq drove you — you have among them not a handspan's worth of foot or hand. They defended the highlands and plains of the Hijaz long ago, and they drove your father from the Sanctuary. How many among them are there of generous lords and of those who do good when they intend or determine. Whenever I call upon Aws and Uthman, war-kindlers come to me — all of them steadfast lords.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place name in the Arabian Peninsula
striations, lines like pen-marks on stone
the back molars, the wisdom teeth
belonging to Khandaq/Khandaf, a major tribal lineage
those who kindle war, fierce warriors

context: A qasida of tribal boasting and self-assertion, in which Ka'b boasts of his descent from his famous father Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma, praises his tribe, and defends himself against a detractor by invoking his family's glory and his clan's martial history.

titleAr: يقول حياي من عوف ومن جشم
titleEn: My kinfolk from Awf and Jusham say to me

verses.sadr: يقول حياي من عوف ومن جشم
verses.ajuz: يا كعب ويجك هلا تشتري غنما

verses.sadr: ما لي منها إذا ما أزمة أزمت
verses.ajuz: ومن أويس إذا ما أنفه رذما
عاري الأشاجع لا يشوي إذا ضغما
أشلاء برد ولم يجعل لها وضما

verses.sadr: إن يغد في شيعة لم يثنه نهر
verses.ajuz: وان غدا واحدا لا يتقي الظلما
في ظلمة ابن جمير ساور الفطما

verses.sadr: إذ لا تزال فريس أو مغببة
verses.ajuz: صيداء تنشج من دون الدماغ دما

translationEn: My kinfolk from 'Awf and Jusham say to me: O Ka'b, woe to you, why don't you buy sheep? What use are sheep to me when a drought year comes — and from 'Uwais when his nose runs phlegm? I fear for them a hunting creature that holds nothing back — bare-knuckled, not missing when it seizes. When it twists into the meat of the sheep it tears it into cold shreds, leaving no flesh on the bone. If it sets out in a group, no stream turns it back — and if it goes alone, it does not fear the dark. If it prowls but misses a sheep on a night when it attacks the people and the cattle — if it raids and gains nothing — in the darkness of Ibn Jumayr it attacks the suckling young. For there is always a fresh kill or a day-old prey — a hunted thing whose skull drips blood without reaching the brain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: dripping with mucus (of an animal's nose)
prey killed the day before yesterday
to drip, to trickle (blood from a wound)

context: Ka'b's kinsmen urge him to buy sheep as a livelihood, but he explains that a wolf or predator in his area makes sheep-rearing impractical — describing the relentless predator in vivid detail.

titleEn: A blazing noon when its gazelles cannot rest

verses.sadr: نصبت لها وجهي على ظهر لاحب

verses.ajuz: لمن كان يسري وهو بالليل طاسم

verses.sadr: أتى دون ماء الرس باد وحاضر

verses.ajuz: لها بصر ترمي به الغيب ساهم
بما انصب من ماء الخياشيم راذم

verses.sadr: وفي جانب الماء الذي كان يبتغي
verses.ajuz: به الري دباب إلى الصيد عالم

verses.sadr: فلما ارتدى جلا من الليل هاجها

verses.ajuz: إذا لم يصب صيدا من الوحش غارم
من الريش ما التفت عليه القوادم
كما أرزمت بكر على البو رائم
زوى سهمه عاو من الجن صارم

verses.sadr: وقال ألا في خيبة أنت من يد

verses.ajuz: نوى عضه من تمر قران عاجم
لها واسق ينجو بها الليل غانم
ولا واسقا ما لم تخنه القوائم

translationEn: A blazing noon when its gazelles cannot settle — its landmarks crowned by the mirage with turbans. You see the dust-colored kicking gazelles in it as if fire has scorched their shanks and burned them. I faced it head-on, riding the back of a worn plain — its pebbles ground fine, smoothed by hooves. You can see it when it climbs the ridges — clear for one who travels and in night wanders dark. I urged upon it a free-flanked swift she-camel, raised on swift feet like tent-poles. You would think the bright of her flank and sides was tar-stain thickened by the pitch-cauldrons. The pebbles of the hard ground scatter between her strides when her matching legs spring and throw. Scattering like a merchant's coins he flicks with his thumbs above his fingers. As if I dressed the saddle on a four-year-old roaming stallion, enclosed by the valley of Jaba and the gullies. He came before the water of al-Rass, nomad and settled, with the brimming deep pools within reach. He turned aside and by al-Salil at midday was as if a man's stripped prey standing high on an eminence. He turns his head to sounds and smells, a leader — his arrow-shaft rubbed smooth by bite-marks on the wood. Deep-sunken in the curve, his orbit's dwelling — he has a gaze that shoots the unseen, fixed. A head like a merchant's packed male ass — as if a thrower has hurled boulders at his brows. His mouth is like the back of a saddle-frame that has betrayed its nails, its bending aggravated. Both his nostrils breathing and scattered, dripping with the flow from the nasal cavities. They stand waiting for his decision, themselves the guided ones for the well, steady. On the side of the water he was seeking to quench his thirst — a crawler expert in hunting. Behind him a hide-crouching listener — long-fasting, experienced, knowing. Expert at arranging flat stones — no wild she-animal escapes him when he is awake on his watch. When a cleared-sky night drove it to the confused pool where the serpents were imprisoned — when it came near the water it smelt the troughs, and the coward feared his death where he stood. They met with it until when its legs tilted downward, he aimed at it as it was drinking and distracted. Exhausted from the midday journey, as if it was a man just recovered from a fever left by Salama. Subtle as the echo of the rock — no wild she-beast beguiles him at a watching point when he is decisive. A man of hides — always as if when he misses game from the wild beasts, he is a debtor. He turns over insects and the fletcher selects from the feathers what the leading-feathers have wrapped. They left full from the points of hard rock, vomiting and dripping the fast-flowing venom. And a yellow bow — its frame tightened by the wood on the dew and moisture, dark red, hidden in color. When its midsection was bent it hummed like a young she-camel lowing over the stuffed hide. He aimed it in the darkness of night, a sturdy coat for their rumps, until the water came — obliged. When he intended the shot on a day and he aimed — a cunning jinn-voice deflected his arrow, sharp. It passed over the smooth sinew of the forearms — the hillocks hardly slowed them. It passed by the sides of both hands — his shaft, and sometimes death turns away from the soul. He bit his thumb-tips in regret, and secretly mourned for his mother while regretting. He said: indeed what a failure, from a hand — and a cutter has severed your fingertips by this last strike. He rose seeking his blade and his shaft — two separate things — while he was stricken with grief and dazed. A male wild ass brayed at them, his hooves like the stones of date-pits, bitten hard. He turned back and by morning at the Sistar was as if a man expelled from men, fasting on a high ground. Unhurried in his deliberateness, yet set on his path — as if he is a loader for her, escaping with her by night as a winner. He swerved left around a pot of the north and a wide flat stone — her landmarks and mountain-passes flanked him. He headed for the water of al-Rasis and it descended toward Layna — the floating stars fell. I have not seen a loaded beast of less stumbling, nor a loader of the like — as long as his legs do not betray him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she-camels or gazelles kicking their legs
a well-worn road or flat plain
a hide or blind used by hunters
the middle section of a bow
the thick base of a tail (or bend of a valley)

context: Ka'b's most extensive nature-description poem: a complete hunt narrative set during blazing midday heat, following a wild ass to water, the hidden archer's failed shot, and the prey's escape — a masterpiece of classical Arabic descriptive poetry.

titleAr: تقول ابنتي ألهى أبي حب أرضه
titleEn: My daughter says: my father is distracted by love of his land

verses.sadr: تقول ابنتي ألهى أبي حب أرضه
بل الهى أباها أنه في عصابة

translationEn: My daughter says: my father has been distracted by his love of his land and his fondness for staying in it. No — what has distracted her father is that he is in a band at Rahman, where the sick among them cannot be visited. They drank a water from lands that is like the blood of vipers — it does not cure one bitten by a snake. The venom of serpents — when they drank it, its crawling and its hissing spread among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a band, a group of men
the crawling of snake venom through the body
the hissing or crawling motion of a snake

context: A brief personal poem in which Ka'b responds to his daughter's concern about his absence, explaining he is with companions in a dangerous place where the water itself is like venom — possibly referring to a military campaign or tribal expedition.

titleEn: From the ruin of the abode desolate for years

verses.sadr: إذا ما انتحت ذات ضغن له

verses.ajuz: وقد كن يأجن أو كن جونا
ن أن لا دخال وأن لا عطونا
دنون من الري أو قد روينا
ولم يك ذاك له الفعل دينا

translationEn: From the ruin of the abode desolate for years — I wept and remained sorrowful and grieving. The wind has dragged its skirts across it until nothing of its trace remains visible. When I saw that weeping is foolishness at worn ruins — I urged what grief lay in my heart and disobeyed my flowing tears. Whenever worries beset me I would burden with them a trustworthy she-camel of thick sinew. A massive, free-flanked one — not a stumbler, nor bearing hidden malice, persistent. As if I strapped to her saddle-ropes a two-year-old wild male ass, bony and lean. He turns around she-asses you see all of them as if they have conceived and hidden a foetus. He denied them water and the blowing of sand disturbed them and stirred them — when they thirsted. He led them astray from the small pools of the shallow dunes — they were not going to drink from the flowing spring. They put the Qanan on the left armpit of the north, and the water of al-'Inab they made on their right. They moved step by step between the near reaches of the ghada-wood and 'Unayza, at a slow pace. They left from it — and the pursuit left a gaunt belly and a thick back. And curved-backed, light legs, sound of sinew — and a firm, solid high rocky hill. When his burst of speed came upon them, you saw folds at his haunches. He bit them with the bite of a press on the straight spears until they softened. He bit the rumps frowning — by his running fierceness they protected themselves. When a resentful one made for him, he squealed — and he drew from her her resentments. He has behind their backs a ringing sound — in the place of a guardian to the archer-gamblers. The forearm's length gasps from among them — and they strike his nose and his forehead. He watered them from the brimming pools — though they had grown stagnant or had turned dark. They stirred up dust over his face the color of chimney-smoke above the hearths. They drank from a cold one — they knew there was no crowding of their watering and no being held back. The frogs expelled their breaths — they climbed just above the water's edge. They encountered one of pressing resentment clinging like the sticking of pot-encrustation, harboring suspicions. Short-fingered, thin of shank — he said: will they come or will they not come? On a cloudy day, hopefully — fatally hitting the vital spots solidly. They came and felt dread at his presence — and did not know for certain any men there. Placing their feet in the cold water — pleasant to taste — they drank in sips. They rushed the gulps, taking turns at them like the striking of a bucket's rope against the well's pebbles. He waited, watching — until when they drew near satiety or were sated — he took up his yellow bow of nab'-wood, on the palm combining rigidity and suppleness. Prepared on its grip, sharpened — its two blade-edges honed, fine-pointed. He released an arrow on a vertebra while they were exposed, not guarding themselves. It passed through its throat and foreleg — and that act was not a debt it owed. He lamented the anguish of his mother — and they turned away wrapped in the rising dust. Their hooves moved easily over the pebbles, and the solid rocks were struck by them in flight. The evening hours stirred them faster than the chest of one returning from watering. He shed and expelled their fur, tracking them ridge by ridge. In the expanse you would think his neighing was a reckless drunk singer among those intoxicated. He lay at dawn in the valley-trees cut down, and they settled down calm together.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ruins of a campsite blackened by fire and dung
small pools of water, shallow waterholes
cloudy, overcast day (providing cover for a hunter)
the inner bend of a valley with trees

context: A long poem in the mutaqarib meter — a rarity for Ka'b — opening with ruins and grief then transitioning into an extended hunt narrative in which a wild male ass drives his herd to water and an archer waits in ambush. The poem is remarkable for its sustained animal description.

titleEn: Come to us, clan of Bahtha — for the land
translationEn: Come to us, clan of Bahtha — for this land: we do not consider it ominous, nor do we forbid it. Come to Dhubyan — for its lands are fortresses and the straight spears are their horns. Do not let me find you encamped on a peak at Tathlith — you being its army and its subjects.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the clan of Bahtha, a branch of Ghatafan
to consider a place ominous, to avoid it as ill-omened
Ka'b's tribe, a major branch of Ghatafan
a straight, hard spear (attributed to the people of Samhar)
a mountain peak, a high summit

context: A brief poem inviting the clan of Bahtha to join Dhubyan, Ka'b's tribe, asserting tribal pride and warning them not to settle in subjugation at Tathlith but rather to come under the protection of the proud Dhubyan.

titleEn: She came to me early at dawn to rebuke me

verses.sadr: ولقد حفظت وصاة من هو ناصح
أرعى الأمانة لا أخون ولا أرى

translationEn: She came to me early at dawn to rebuke me — and that is sufficient as ignorance and rashness of tongue. I have kept the counsel of one who is sincere to me, knowing the vulnerable spots of friends. Until when the bones grew lean I drove her off — the driving of one jealous of his honor, angered. I saw her tire from fear of being worn down by me — and she rushed away, at what a time! You have known, though you are not forbearing, that desire never brings me close to humiliation. May your mother mourn you — do you have in these last days any clarification to offer that might guide? I keep trust — I do not betray, and I am never seen frequenting the yard of the treacherous. You became strange to me after an established love — how can the bond of one of changing colors reconcile? One day yielding to the lead, another time you meet it estranged from aversion. Sometimes you meet it as your brother, other times you meet it and think it is from the Sudanese. A desolate plain-sickness — whose evil the cautious fears — its terrors are close to calamities. A dust-colored, low-landmarked stretch — I crossed it at night on a concealer of travel, compliant. A lean she-camel stretching her halter with a massive beast — like a trunk whose lush fiber has been stripped. Angry with her foot-pad — the pebbles cry at her like the blow of an axe on a green branch. She raises her gaze at figures — alert, with the wild sharpness of instinct. Sunken-eyed, clear — giving freely of her sweat in the midday heat like the dripping of a thirsty one. She repels the midday heat and the dust with a brow — like a cave guarded before it by a wall of stones. Bright-eyed — her gaze wanders above her at the halting-place as the tick-gatherer passes at night. Her forelegs gave out on him — as if his feet grow on smooth rock. She strutted and challenged young she-camels with sunken eyes and lowered chins. I likened her to a white-backed, dapple-legged creature — gaunt of belly. He set off with two straight ones — neither stripped of their fine form, no crookedness in them nor roughness. Both of them under the mist — as if the straightener has oiled their smooth surfaces with oil. He set off with alert ears — He who created ears gave them caution and acute hearing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: at the pre-dawn hour, at early morning
the vulnerable spots, the points of weakness
one jealous of his honor, protective

context: A qasida beginning with Ka'b's wife reproaching him at dawn, his firm response asserting his trustworthiness and pride, then transitioning into a description of a lean she-camel and a wild ass with two horns — the typical three-section classical ode structure.
